content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1130 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

October 28, 1977

77-10-073

Mr. Robert K. Booth, Jr.
Palo Alto City Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue
Palo Alto, California 94301

Dear Mr. Booth:

This is in response to your letter of 19 October 1977
regarding the participation by Councilmember Ann Witherspoon
in various actions of the Palo Alto City Council concerning
the Sand Hill Road Improvement Project. In my letter to you
dated 2 September to which you refer in your letter, I reit-
erated the staff's view that Ms. Witherspoon should not, in
light of Government Code §87100, "make or participate in the
making" of any decisions which, in light of all the surround-
ing circumstances, call into question the continued viability
of the project. You ask for clarification of that advice in
the context of certain specific decisions which the Council
must make at its meeting on 7 November, and ask whether Ms.
Witherspoon may legally engage in the making of these deci-
sions.

It appears to us that you have answered your own question.
To wit, on page two of your letter you say:

We wish to emphasize that approval or
disapproval of any of the foregoing
actions particularly those pertaining
to the assessment district directly,
will have a material financial effect
upon the university.

To the extent this is the case, we think it clear that
Government Code §§87100 and 87103, as summarized and inter-
preted in my letters to you dated 3 August and 2 September,
prohibit Ms. Witherspoon from participating in those decisions.

Robert K. Booth, Jr.
October 28, 1977
Page Two


We are aware that the matter in question appears to be a
sensitive one subject to considerable debate and feeling pro
and con. However, we also think the Government Code and pre-
vious interpretations of same rendered by the Commission pur-
suant to §83114 dictate the cited conclusion.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest
Division


KWG:glb

City of Palo Alto
CALIFORNIA
94301

CIVIC CENTER
250 HAMILTON AVENUE
PALO ALTO
CALIFORNIA 94301
(415) 329-2171

October 19, 1977


Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist III
Conflicts of Interest Division
State of California Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Mr. Goshorn:

On September 2, 1977, you advised this office by letter regarding participation by Councilmember Ann Witherspoon in various actions of the Palo Alto City Council with respect to the Sand Hill Road Improvement Project. Unfortunately, there continues to exist some confusion in our mind as to whether or not she may participate in the forthcoming actions required on the project. Accordingly, following is further information which we hope you will consider and give us your advice at the earliest opportunity. The next series of actions relating to this project is scheduled for Monday, November 7, 1977 and we would appreciate receipt of your opinion prior to that time.

Summarizing events to date, Stanford University is a non-profit corporation and not a business entity as that term is defined in Government Code Section 82005. Accordingly, to determine whether the requisite financial interest exist only the "source of income" test would be applicable under Section 87103(C). Mrs. Witherspoon is employed by Stanford Medical Center, which is a separate legal entity owned by and under the control of the Board of Trustees of the University. Her activities are primarily related to private charitable fund raising for the benefit of various medical activities.

The road construction project involved will cost in excess of $6,000,000, with approximately seventy-five percent to be assessed to Stanford University. The university in turn, however, will pass along a substantial portion of its assessments to various lessees, including a large apartment project, the Stanford Shopping Center lessees and other commercial type uses. Some of the assessments will be borne by the university or by its medical activities. Moreover, creation

of the assessment district will provide an immediate cash pay-
ment to the university, which will be repaid over time as the
assessment district bonds are paid off in the manner set forth
above. It is our understanding that you have concluded that
the project will have a "material financial effect" upon the
source of income of Mrs. Witherspoon.

Your letter of September 2nd states, "We believe that she is
only prohibited from making or participating in decisions
which, in light of all this surrounding circumstances,
realistically involved the continued viability of the project."
It is this language which we believe needs further clarification.

On November 7, 1977, the Council will have before it for
consideration a number of actions relating to the project.
The failure of any one of these to be approved by the City
Council will result in termination of the assessment district,
although the project could conceivably proceed forward if
alternative ways to finance it are found. It is notable that
the university is under a legal obligation pursuant to a
contract executed some years ago to build the project upon
the request of the City, whether the same is financed through
an assessment district or not.

On November 7, 1977, the City Council will receive and hear any
written protests to the project and will thereafter consider a
resolution overruling the same. It will then consider a
resolution ordering the project to proceed, including acquisition
of property and construction of the improvements and also will
consider a resolution confirming the proposed spread of the
assessment upon the various parcels of property and will
consider one or more proposed agreements with the university
relating to restrictions on certain uses of remaining property,
establishing a setback from a creek channel and construction
of an additional street on the campus itself which is not a
part of the assessment district project. There are some other
actions with the Council also will consider, but these are
of a lesser nature.

We wish to emphasize that approval or disapproval of any of the
foregoing actions particularly those pertaining to the assess-
ment district directly, will have a material financial effect
upon the university.

It is also possible that the City Council may decide not to
proceed forward with the project itself, regardless of the
financing matters at issue in the assessment district, and
irrespective of the university's preexisting obligation to
construct the road upon request.

Because this project is of considerable significance to both
the university and the City, we wish to be certain that the

-2-

Commission Staff has been fully apprised of all of the aspects
of it before issuing its opinion letter. Accordingly, we
ask you to once again consider this matter and give us your
advice. Please contact me if you need further information
on any of the material discussed herein.

Sincerely,

[signature]
ROBERT K. BOOTH, JR.
City Attorney

RKB:jj


-3-

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Palo Alto City Councilmember Ann Witherspoon may participate in upcoming City Council votes on the Sand Hill Road Improvement Project given her employment at Stanford Medical Center, which would be materially affected financially by the project's approval or denial.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that Councilmember Witherspoon should not participate in decisions that realistically involve the continued viability of the project, as the project will have a material financial effect upon Stanford University (her employer's parent entity) and thus upon the source of her income under Government Code Section 87103(c).
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is heavily degraded with significant OCR errors and illegible sections. The response letter from FPPC (dated October 19, 1977) appears as an incoming request/follow-up rather than the original FPPC advice. The actual FPPC response letters dated October 19 and October 28, 1977 are substantially illegible. Confidence is reduced due to document quality and fragmentation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Palo Alto City Councilmember Ann Witherspoon may participate in upcoming City Council votes on the Sand Hill Road Improvement Project given her employment at Stanford Medical Center, which would be materially affected financially by the project's approval or denial.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advises that Councilmember Witherspoon should not participate in decisions that realistically involve the continued viability of the project, as the project will have a material financial effect upon Stanford University (her employer's parent entity) and thus upon the source of her income under Government Code Section 87103(c).

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC advice letter responding to a Palo Alto City Attorney's request for clarification regarding conflict of interest restrictions on a city councilmember's participation in votes concerning a major road improvement project that would materially affect her employer, Stanford University.
